# Transcrição: 03-Avadaco Melt

- **Fonte:** 03-Avadaco Melt
- **Duração:** 06:02
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

I'm standing in the kitchen. I'm holding the very first avocado of my life. And it's not because I'm sick, and I've never had the opportunity, or it's not because I'm living in a foreign land, and avocados are not available to me at this time. It's because I am a grown-ass man who has spent his entire life eating cheeseburgers and twizzlers and poptarts. And this is a foreign object to me. I am a 32-year-old man child. I am standing in my cruddy kitchen in my cruddy apartment, honestly trying to figure out how to open an avocado. And I need to open it because the woman who I love, who I hope will someday love me, is coming. I'm making the first meal for her ever in this kitchen, and I'm holding something that I can't open, that I know everyone else can open. And it's my attempt to be something that I'm not. Ever since we began dating two months ago, I've been erecting this facade to show her something that I am not. I'm desperately trying not to be me. And so I've done things like I bought a shower curtain so that the bathroom does not shower with me. And I changed my sheets for the first time ever. I bought candles, not because the apartment smells bad, but I just feel like it's the adult thing to do. And I got a haircut, and my dog got a haircut. I am doing everything I can so she can see me be the me I want to be instead of the me that I am forced to be. And that means right now I am going to make an actual meal for a girl walking in the door who I want to love someday and I want her to love me. But I can't get an avocado open. So I do the one thing that I am good at. I call for help. I call my friend Justine, and I tell her, Justine, I'm trying to make a meal so I can trick a woman into believing I'm something I'm not so that she can marry that thing that she thinks I am and then live with that thing forever. Can you help me? And when she's done laughing, she explains to me that I need to get a knife. And so I get a knife, and she explains to me how to cut this thing open. There's a pit in the middle, by the way. You don't need it. You just throw it away. So I cut the avocado open, and I describe what I see inside, because I've never seen the inside of an avocado before. And she tells me the truth of the avocado, which is avocados are only ripe for about 19 seconds of their existence. She tells me that this avocado is not ripe. And she said, didn't you squeeze it in the grocery store? I'm like, stop it, stop it. Don't talk about my past failures. Let's deal with my failures in the moment and leave the past in the past. I say, what can I do? Can I microwave it? Can I put it in hot water? How do I do this? And she says, you're screwed. There's nothing you can do. Go get another one. And I said, I can't. She's about to arrive. And literally, there's a knock on the door.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

And I hear Justine say, yes, you are screwed. So I answer the door, and she's there. And she is as perfect as she has ever been. And I just feel so awful that I could not make her a meal. And it's an avocado melt that I'm trying to make. It is a grilled cheese with a little avocado on top. That is my failure. And so she comes in, and I tell her what I've done, and I point to my disgrace in the kitchen. And there's an avocado on a cutting board. There's a lemon, too, that I was supposed to zest. I had no idea what a zest was. I hadn't even gotten to that. And she laughs. And she goes in to look at my mess. And then she picks up a piece of paper next to the cutting board, and she says, what's this? Is this a recipe? And I say, yeah. And she looks closely at it, and she says, is this Martin's recipe? And I say, yeah. About a month before, we were at Martin's, a restaurant in the Berkshire, a place she had been going all her life. And while we were having breakfast that morning, she said that the avocado melt at Martin's is the greatest avocado melt in the world. So when I got home that night, I called Martin's to get the recipe, which was like 16 years ago, which was a thing, because that's not like take a picture and text it to me. That is, use a phone as a phone, like hold it to your head, and talk to a stranger, and convince them to take a menu, and photocopy, like zoom, zoom, and put it in an envelope, and mail it to me. It was a thing. And now it's here on the counter, and now she's holding it. And she's holding the menu, the recipe of the avocado melt, and I watch her melt. Like she smiles and starts to cry at the same time. And I don't understand it first, because this is something I can do. I can use the phone, and I can call people, and I can convince them to mail things. But this mess in front of her, that's my failure. And she walks over to me, and she kisses me in my kitchen. And it's the moment I understand that when you find the right person, you don't need to be a success. You just have to try to be a success. You have to listen. You have to remember. And you have to love. That's all that she wanted from me that day, not the food, but just knowing that I saw her and knew her and listened to her. And so we have been married for 13 years. And I have tried three other times to make my wife an avocado melt. And I have failed every time. But every morning I wake up, she does not divorce me. Somehow she has married that facade that I created that in the end, all she wanted was me to be me. I didn't make to make anything for her. I don't need to do anything special for her. I just need to try like hell every day to keep her loving me. Thank you. Thank you.
